coredata.dc:title: Properties of fluorescent chiral carbon microspheres and nanodots synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Wei-Li
Chuang, Yi-Chen
Chau, Chi-Min
Lei, Chien-Ming
Su, Chi-Jung
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon microspheres (CMSs) and carbon nanodots (CNDs) synthesized together in one pot have received much attention in the past decade. Here we report the use of L- and D-ascorbic acids (AA) to prepare CMSs with different chirality and CNDs with different fluorescent emission by the hydrothermal method. The CMSs emit a blue light while the CNDs emit a green one under 365 nm UV light. The reaction conversions of CMSs prepared with this method are over 25% when the reaction temperature is above 180°C. Circular dichroism, specific rotation, and photoluminescence of CMSs were measured. The analyses show that the L- and D-AA-based CMSs exhibit different chiral spectra. The graphene-like structures of CMSs are revealed by Raman spectroscopy. A possible formation mechanism of the CMSs and CNSs is proposed. In addition, the CMSs after calcination were found to achieve higher electrical and thermal conductivities comparable to those of the commercial carbon black. Photoluminescence analyses of CNDs show that the relative fluctuation between the blue and green emissions determines the shifting of blue and green hybrid color of the synthesized CNDs. D-AA derived CNDs show a higher quantum yield (QY) close to 1% and a stronger green emission. The blue-emitting CNDs and the green-emitting ones could be separated with the larger green CNDs kept inside the dialysis tube.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon microspheres
Carbon nanodots
Hydrothermal method
Fluorescence
Chirality
Conductivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedures for the hydrothermal synthesis of FCCMSs and CND
Effects of reaction parameters on conversion and size
Electrical and thermal properties of FCCMSs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science and Technology of R.O.C.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the Ministry of Science and Technology of R.O.C. (109-2218-E-035-004-) and Feng Chia University ( 09G27205 and 12G27154 ) for financial support. Also appreciated is the Precision Instrument Support Center of Feng Chia University for providing the microscope facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of FCCMSs prepared under condition 1 (a), condition 2 (b), condition 3 and 4 (c), and condition 5 (d). TEM image of FCCMSs prepared under condition 2 (e).
(a) CD spectra of FCCMSs made of L- and D-AAs. (b) IR spectra of FCCMSs made of L-AA.
Absorption (UV/Vis) and photoluminescence (under 300 nm) spectra of FCCMSs.

Top: illuminated CNDs solutions under ambient light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right), prepared with D-AA (a) and L-AA (b) by hydrothermal reaction at 150°C for 1 h. Bottom: photoluminescent spectra of CNDs made of D-AA (c) and L-AA (d), each exited by 330, 350, 360, and 370 nm UV light.

Top: illuminated CNDs solutions under ambient light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right), prepared with D-AA (a) and L-AA (b) by hydrothermal reaction at 180°C for 1 h. Bottom: photoluminescent spectra of CNDs made of D-AA (c) and L-AA (d), each exited by 330, 350, 360, and 370 nm UV light.

Top: illuminated CNDs solutions under ambient light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right), prepared with D-AA (a) and L-AA (b) by hydrothermal reaction at 180°C for 3 h. Bottom: photoluminescent spectra of CNDs made of D-AA (c) and L-AA (d), each exited by 330, 350, 360, and 370 nm UV light.

Simple EMI shielding test. (a) CMSs first laminated in two sheets of PARAFILM and further sandwiched between two glass dishes. Thickness = 0.22 cm; area density = 27.1 mg/cm2; and distance from EMI source to detector = 1.72 cm. (b) Temperature controller of an oven in operation as an EMI source. (c) Unshielded results of test. (d) Shielded results of test.
Effects of reaction temperature and time on size of FCCMSs.
Comparison of measured electrical conductivities (σ) and thermal conductivities (κ) of the as prepared and calcined FCCMSs with those of the commercial carbon black.
Comparison of measured quantum yields (QYs) of the prepared fluorescent CNDs under different hydrothermal temperature and time combinations.

head.ce:title: Properties of fluorescent chiral carbon microspheres and nanodots synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: 110, Sec. 1, Jianguo N. Rd., South Dist.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon microspheres (CMSs) and carbon nanodots (CNDs) synthesized together in one pot have received much attention in the past decade. Here we report the use of L- and D-ascorbic acids (AA) to prepare CMSs with different chirality and CNDs with different fluorescent emission by the hydrothermal method. The CMSs emit a blue light while the CNDs emit a green one under 365 nm UV light. The reaction conversions of CMSs prepared with this method are over 25% when the reaction temperature is above 180°C. Circular dichroism, specific rotation, and photoluminescence of CMSs were measured. The analyses show that the L- and D-AA-based CMSs exhibit different chiral spectra. The graphene-like structures of CMSs are revealed by Raman spectroscopy. A possible formation mechanism of the CMSs and CNSs is proposed. In addition, the CMSs after calcination were found to achieve higher electrical and thermal conductivities comparable to those of the commercial carbon black. Photoluminescence analyses of CNDs show that the relative fluctuation between the blue and green emissions determines the shifting of blue and green hybrid color of the synthesized CNDs. D-AA derived CNDs show a higher quantum yield (QY) close to 1% and a stronger green emission. The blue-emitting CNDs and the green-emitting ones could be separated with the larger green CNDs kept inside the dialysis tube.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Chiral functional materials have theoretical and practical importance in many areas. In chemistry and biology, chirality has applications in enantiomeric separation, pharmaceutics, and nanotechnology [1–4]. Further, the preparation of such functional materials from renewable resources has drawn much attention in view of energy and resource sustainability [5–7].

Traditionally carbonaceous materials are prepared by pyrolysis-based methods such as chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [8–10]. We have reported producing carbon microspheres (CMSs) by catalytic CVD using acetylene (C2H2) as carbon source and a setting between 700 and 800°C as reaction temperature [11]. In recent years, hollow, solid, and core-shell carbon microspheres have been prepared by hydrothermal synthesis with potential applications in electromagnetic wave shielding [12–15], solar cell [16–18], electrochemical sensor [19], composite [20], energy/mass storage [21–23], catalysis [24], and (bio-) separation and purification [25–27]. The carbon precursors commonly used in CMSs preparation were mono/poly-saccharides found in fruit and fat. Simpler compounds such as furfural and other degraded products from biomass, have also been used [28–32]. The hydrothermal treatment of carbohydrates or biomass offers an inexpensive and green way to produce functional carbon materials.

L-ascorbic acid (L-AA, vitamin C) is a sugar acid and has been widely used as antioxidant and nutrition additive. In our previous works, L-AA has been used to prepare CMSs to blend with an electrically conducting polymer (ECP) [33]. Through tunneling and percolation among the AA-based CMSs distributed in the ECP matrix, an enhancement of electrical conductivity of the composite was achieved. Regarding the chiral function, Deng et al reported the preparation of chiral and fluorescent polymer microspheres about 1 μm using substituted acetylene monomers [34]. So far, the optical activity of the AA-based CMSs, inherited from the chirality of AAs, has not yet been investigated. Since there are two chiral centers and four hydroxyl groups on L-AA to render the molecule chiral, it is thus possible to prepare chiral CMSs (CCMSs) using vitamin C and its enantiomers. Although synthesis of nanometer sized carbon nanoparticles in the presence of ethanol with vitamin C has been earlier reported [35], our method is ethanol free and our CCMSs are simply comprised of carbon nanodots. The large size of CCMSs has the advantage in packing columns for enantiomeric separation.

Along with the micro-sized CMSs, the nano-sized carbon nanodots (CNDs) are always the couple product. CMSs often come from the carbonized precipitate while CNDs exist in the solution supernatant [36,37]. In general, water-soluble carbon nanodots exhibit fluorescent properties when prepared by the hydrothermal method using vitamin C, carboxymethylcellulose, pericarp, or even beer as carbon source. Quantum yields about CNDs have been reported a lot but few for CMSs. One study has reported a QY about 0.95% for the photoluminescent CMSs [38]. In this work, we measured the QYs of our FCCMSs and the accompanied CNDs prepared by a one-step hydrothermal process.

We report here that both L- and D-AAs can serve as carbon and chirality source for synthesizing FCCMSs (and the co-product CNDs) in a one-step hydrothermal process. A mechanism of forming FCCMSs was first proposed. Optical activity of FCCMSs was characterized by measuring the circular dichroism (CD), specific rotation, and fluorescence. Electrical and thermal properties were also measured.

All the chemicals used were of analytical grade. L- and D-AAs were 99% in purity and obtained from Union Chemical Works Ltd. (Taiwan). L-AA 6-palmitate was 99% in purity and obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). The conductive carbon black Vulcan XC-72 was purchased from Cabot (USA). For quantum yield calculation of FCCMSs, POPOP (1,4-bis(5-phenyloxazol-2-yl)benzene, 98+%) was acquired from Alfa Aesar. POPOP has a QY equal to 97% in cyclohexane when excited at 300 nm. The deionized water used to prepare the samples was greater than 18 MΩ cm in resistivity and produced by the Roda ultrapure water system (Te Chen, Taiwan). Quinine (99%, Acros Organics) dissolved in aqueous solution of sulfuric acid (0.1 M) was used as the fluorescent standard to measure the QYs of CNDs. Quinine sulfate gives a QY of 54% under 360 nm UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedures for the hydrothermal synthesis of FCCMSs and CND
ce:section.ce:para: A well-stirred 0.6 M solution of L- or D-AA containing 10 mL DI water and 1.0567 g AA was put in a Teflon container and further sealed tightly in a steel autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated according to either of the 5 conditions listed in Table 1 . When the heating was stopped, the autoclave was allowed to cool down to room temperature. As a result, a dark precipitate formed and was collected and washed with DI water. The rinsed product was dried in vacuum at 60°C for 24 h. The reaction conversion was defined as the percent weight ratio of collected FCCMSs to added AA. Moreover, after passing the sample solution through a common filtering paper the filtrate was collected as the CNDs.

The morphology of FCCMSs was characterized using the scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL JSM-5200) and the transmission electron microscope (TEM, Philips FEI Tecnai G2 F20). The PELCO copper grids as TEM sample support were supplied by Ted Pella. The functional groups on the surface of the microspheres were identified with the Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Jasco FTIR-400). The CD analyses were performed on the Jasco J-810 spectropolarimeter. Absorption and fluorescence data were measured with the Cary 50 UV-Vis spectrophotometer and the Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrometer. The electrical conductivity and thermal conductivity of the as-prepared and burned FCCMSs were measured separately by the Keithley 6430 sub-femtoamp source meter and a lab-made system similar to the setup described in ASTM E1225-09.

The mechanism of formation of FCCMSs is proposed in reference to Wechtersbach's research [39]. Wechtersbach et al. reported recently that dehydroascorbic acid (DHA) and dehydroascorbic acid dimer (DDHA) are produced during the hydrolysis of vitamin C and intermolecular dehydration of DHA, respectively. Based on his work, we propose the reasonable formation mechanism of FCCMSs as shown in Fig. 1 .

From Fig. 1 we see that AAs are hydrolyzed upon heating to form DHA which was then converted into DDHA through intermolecular dehydration. Finally, the unstable DDHA was hydrothermally carbonized into FCCMSs. Since L- and D-AAs consist of a γ-lactone ring in which the ene-diol portion and the oxygen of the lactone moiety are conjugated to the carbonyl group [2], they should follow a carbonization route different from those of the carbohydrates without a lactone structure such as glucose and fructose. After CNDs are formed by bond breaking and rearrangement, the CMSs gradually emerged by aggregation and agglomeration of CNDs.

One experiment result worth noting is described as follows. Although the FCCMSs can be synthesized using L-AA as precursor, they cannot be formed by the same process while using L-AA 6-palmitate as precursor. We suppose that the long side chains hinder the formation of the bicyclic structure of DHA and thus no FCCMSs could be formed. The fact supports our proposed mechanism indirectly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of reaction parameters on conversion and size
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 gives the recipes for preparing FCCMSs by the hydrothermal method. Two parameters, reaction temperature and time, were studied in synthesizing FCCMSs. Each product is a dark precipitate of FCCMSs which could be collected, rinsed, and dried for subsequent analyses. The conversions and sizes of the as-prepared FCCMSs under different conditions are also given in Table 1. All the process conditions brought forth a conversion higher than 25% except for condition 1.

Morphologies of FCCMSs prepared under different reaction conditions are shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2a shows that the FCCMSs prepared at 160°C (Table 1, condition 1) consist mainly of small microspheres and irregular-shaped pellets. When the reaction temperature is 180°C (Table 1, condition 2), larger FCCMSs 4.5–5.5 μm in diameter are observed in Fig. 2b.

Besides temperature, the reaction time is also crucial to the morphology of FCCMSs. The FCCMSs become even larger in size (Fig. 2c) when the reaction time is longer than 3 h (Table 1, conditions 3 and 4). When the temperature is above 180°C (Table 1, condition 5), the FCCMSs suffer from serious cementing (Fig. 2d) and the aggregated spheres become smaller in size than those from condition 2 due to the surface diffusion of mass from the spheres to the necks. Regarding the sphere interior, FCCMSs are found solid rather than hollow for all synthesis conditions, proved by the nicks on the spheres observed in the SEM images and the solid cores revealed by the TEM micrograph.

Based on the results and discussion made so far, condition 2 involving 180°C and 3 h is considered to be the transition point for the CMSs to start growing mature. Hence, in the following we continue to discuss the results of the L- and D-AA derived FCCMSs prepared under condition 2.

To demonstrate the optical activity, first the circular dichroism spectra were measured by dispersing dried FCCMSs made from L- and D-AAs in CH3OH. The CD spectra in Fig. 3 a clearly show that the FCCMSs exhibit nontrivial contrast in chirality, resulting from the chiral groups of AAs inherited by the FCCMSs. Second, the average specific rotations of the FCCMSs made of L- and D-AAs were measured as [α]18 D = +164.8 cm3 g−1 dm−1 (c = 10−4 g cm−3 in DMSO) and [α]18 D = +200.2 cm3 g−1 dm−1 (c = 10−4 g cm−3 in DMSO), respectively. Both results demonstrate the discernable optical activities of the FCCMSs synthesized from enantiomeric carbon sources.

In addition, as shown in Fig. 3b, the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra display the characteristic broad band of stretching hydroxyl groups above 3000 cm−1 for both L-AA and its FCCMSs. First, the FCCMSs are rendered hydrophilic by the hydroxyl groups as shown detected by FTIR. Second, lots of the hydroxyl groups on FCCMSs should come from the chiral groups of AA molecules and inherit their chirality.

The absorption and emission spectra of FCCMSs are shown in Fig. 4 . The UV/Vis spectra of the two FCCMSs give similar absorption peaks around 210 and 266 nm. The peak with maximum PL emission falls between 350 and 450 nm and corresponds to violet fluorescence under 300 nm UV excitation. However, the apparent color of the FCCMS solutions under irradiation is cyan because there are a second fluorescent peak about 510 nm, corresponding to green emission. Using POPOP as fluorescence standard and cyclohexane as solvent, the QYs of FCCMSs from L- and D-AAs are calculated as 5.8 and 3.3%, respectively.

From Raman analysis, the FCCMSs prepared using L-AA as carbon and chiral source show one weak peak at around 1350 cm−1 and another strong peak at 1585 cm−1 (Fig. 5 ). The two peaks correspond to the D band and G band of benzene-ring related materials such as graphite and carbon nanotubes. In our case, the D bands and G bands are broadened so our samples more resemble the structures of amorphous carbon films. To compare the two recorded spectra, the FCCMSs prepared using D-AA show a much stronger D band at 1390 cm−1 and a similar but slightly red-shifted G band at 1595 cm−1. The larger intensity ratio ID/IG of CMSs made of D-AA than that made of L-AA is considered related to the differences in fluorescence behavior between the D- and L-AA derived CNDs, which will be discussed later.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical and thermal properties of FCCMSs
ce:section.ce:para: The electrical conductivity (σ) and thermal conductivity (κ) of the as-prepared and burned FCCMSs were measured and compared with those of Vulcan XC72. As shown in Table 2 , the as-prepared FCCMSs are electrically insulating and one order of magnitude smaller in κ than XC72. However, the calcined FCCMSs, treated at 500°C for 2 h in air, give enhanced σ and κ both comparable to those of XC72 due to carbonization of organic groups. The burned FCCMSs became insoluble in water, indicating the loss of hydrophilic groups and chirality. Hence, there is a tradeoff between the optical activity and electrical conductivity.

As shown in the photos of Fig. 6 a and b, yellow colors were observed under ambient light for both CNDs prepared with D-AA and L-AA by hydrothermal reaction at 150°C for 1 h. Bluish green fluorescent colors were observed under 365 nm UV light for both CNDs prepared with D- and L-AAs. However, if we investigate the CNDs solutions further with photoluminescent spectroscopy, a triple-peak spectra could be observed.

Fig. 6c shows the first two peaks sit in the realm between 350 and 450 nm and the third one is located close to 500 nm. The first two peaks contribute mainly to the bluish fluorescence while the third peak, to the greenish fluorescence. With the exciting frequency of UV light increased from 330 to 370 nm, we see a turnover of the dominant peak from blue to green for the D-AA derived CNDs, which indicates the redshift of the emission color. The distinct blue and green parts of fluorescence suggest the existence of different light-emitting groups or sites in those D-AA derived CNDs.

In contract, Fig. 6d shows mild triple-peak spectra with blue portion as the dominant fluorescence. The green peak never outgrows the blue ones for those L-AA derived CNDs. The different fluorescent behavior of the D- and L-AA derived CNDs is considered related to the difference in D-band strength between the D- and L-AA derived FCCMSs.

If we increase the reaction temperature from 150 to 180°C, the photos of Figs. 7 a and 6b show similar yellow colors under ambient light but brighter bluish-green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light for both CNDs prepared with D-AA and L-AA reacting for 1 h. We will see the CNDs solutions still possess a triple-peak spectra in photoluminescence.

Fig. 7c again shows two peaks covering the frequency range from 350 to 450 nm and a third one located in between 450 and 500 nm. The first two peaks merge gradually and shrink relative to the third peak as the exciting UV frequency increases from 330 to 370 nm, resulting in a larger green share in fluorescence regarding those D-AA derived CNDs. The ebbing of the double peaks are attributed to the lowered incident photon energy which renders itself insufficient to excite the electrons to re-emit blue light.
Same as Fig. 6d and 7d shows mild triple-peak spectra with the blue portion as the dominant fluorescence. The green portion does not outweigh the blue one for those L-AA derived CNDs.

If we elongate the reaction time from 1 h to 3 h, the sample solutions in Fig. 8 a and b show similar yellow colors under ambient light. Under 365 nm UV light, we observe greenish cyanic fluorescence for both CNDs prepared with D-AA and L-AA at 180°C.

Under this preparation condition, both Fig. 8c and d mainly show double peaks across the frequency range of 350-450 nm, but still with a green wake around 500 nm. Therefore, the emission is dominantly blue. As the exciting UV frequency increases from 330 to 370 nm, the double peaks in the blue range only red shift the frequency in proper order but either raise or lower the green wake for both the D- and L-AA derived CNDs. That is, these D-AA derived CNDs becomes more like those L-AA derived CNDs which always have a higher blue peak than the green one. The results show, under prolonged hydrothermal action, more chromophores could be created which are excitable by UV 350, 360, and 370 nm such that the double peaks in the blue range do not drop with increased wavelength as quickly as before. Referring to Fig. 4, the larger D band of D-AA derived FCCMSs might represent an increase of those emissive sites. Therefore, both the matured FCCMSs and CNDs exhibit cyanic fluorescence over a range of UV excitation as the hydrothermal time passes 3 h.

The calculated QYs of the CNDs solutions prepared in this study are listed in Table 3 . There are three trends we can roughly deduce based on the data. First we see D-AA derived CNDs have larger QYs than those of L-AA derived ones. Second, hydrothermal reactions taking place at a higher temperature give higher QYs. Third, hydrothermal reactions taking place for a longer time help enhance QYs. However, a lower temperature or a shorter reaction time may render an opportunity to tune the blue to green emission ratio of the CNDs made of D-AA. Care should be taken when handling CNDs with multi-peak fluorescent spectra in order to reach a desired hue of the fluorescence.

Finally, based on the electrical conductance property of FCCMSs, we performed a simple test on shielding external electromagnetic waves (EMWs) to reduce possible EM interference (EMI) using our calcined CMSs. As shown in Fig. 9 a, a CMSs thin film was prepared and used to intervene between the EMW source and the detector. The thin film of CMSs was found able to reduce the strength of both the electrical and magnetic fields of the EMWs passing through it, indicating of the shielding capability of our sample. In addition to EMI shielding, the ability of any free or excited elections in a material to interact with external EMWs or photons could play a role in photocatalysis and photoluminescence. Fig. 9b shows we took the temperature controller of an oven in operation as the EMI source. Fig. 9c shows the unshielded results giving an electrical field over 1200 V/m and a magnetic field -2.9 µT, using an electromagnetic radiation tester (GM3120, Benetech, China). Fig. 9d shows the shielded results with the electrical field dropping below 600 V/m and the magnetic field reduced to -2.6 µT.

In conclusion, we have presented here the preparation of CMSs and CNDs together from L- and D-AAs using a green and one-step hydrothermal synthesis. The Raman patterns show that the FCCMSs derived from D-AA possess a larger D band than that from L-AA. The larger D band results from the long hydrothermal time over 3 h which aids in forming more excitable blue-emitting sites on CNDs. In addition, the novel optical properties associated with CMSs and CNDs are unique and will have applications in luminescent inks [40], microwave absorbing materials, and cell biology [41]. The calcined FCCMSs are electrically/thermally conducting and will find applications in catalyst support, battery electrode, solar cell, and heavy metal ion adsorbent.

Wei-Li Yuan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yi-Chen Chuang: Data curation, Visualization, Investigation. Chi-Min Chau: Writing – original draft. Chien-Ming Lei: Resources, Data curation. Chi-Jung Su: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Ministry of Science and Technology of R.O.C. (109-2218-E-035-004-) and Feng Chia University (09G27205 and 12G27154) for financial support. Also appreciated is the Precision Instrument Support Center of Feng Chia University for providing the microscope facilities.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enantioselective separation of amino acids using chiral polystyrene microspheres synthesized by a post-polymer modification approach

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemical synthesis of carbon materials with intriguing nanostructure and morphology

Amabilino D.B., Veciana J. Supramolecular chiral functional materials. In: Crego-Calama M., Reinhoudt D.N., (eds). Supramol. Chirality, Berlin, Heidelberg: Springer Berlin Heidelberg; 2006, p. 253–302.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile functionalization of HTC-derived carbon microspheres

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A self-assembled hierarchical nanostructure comprising carbon spheres and graphene nanosheets for enhanced supercapacitor performance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Guo C.X., Li C.M. A self-assembled hierarchical nanostructure comprising carbon spheres and graphene nanosheets for enhanced supercapacitor performance. Energy Environ. Sci. 2011;4:4504–7.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and conductivity measurement of carbon spheres by catalytic CVD using non-magnetic metal complexes

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple route to prepare Fe3O4@C microspheres as electromagnetic wave absorbing material
